section.title: Let All Creation Praise the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The third Hallelujah psalm summons all creation, heavens and earth alike, angels, sun, moon, stars, sea creatures, weather, mountains, trees, animals, and every class of human being, to praise the LORD who created and sustains them all, and who has raised up a horn, a rescuer king, for His people, foreshadowing Revelation 5:13's vision of every creature praising the Lamb.
section.thesis: All creation, from the highest heavens to the depths of the sea, exists to praise its Maker, and its worship is fulfilled when creation's Redeemer, who raised up a horn of salvation for His people, receives, with all creation, unending praise.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the heavens summoned to praise, verses 1-6; the earth summoned to praise, verses 7-10; and every class of humanity summoned to praise, culminating in the horn raised up for Israel, verses 11-14.

units.label: Praise Him, all His angels
units.note: the heavens summoned to praise their Creator

verses.text: {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}! Praise the LORD from the heavens; praise Him in the highest places.
Praise Him, all His angels; praise Him, all His heavenly {{tsava|hosts}}.
Praise Him, O sun and moon; praise Him, all you shining stars.
Praise Him, O highest heavens, and you waters above the skies.
Let them praise the name of the LORD, for He gave the command and they were created.
He established them forever and ever; He issued a decree that will never pass away.

units.label: Praise the Lord from the earth
units.note: sea creatures, weather, mountains, trees, and animals summoned to praise

verses.text: Praise the LORD from the earth, all great sea creatures and ocean depths,
lightning and hail, snow and clouds, powerful wind fulfilling His word,
mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars,
wild animals and all cattle, crawling creatures and flying birds,

units.label: Let them praise the name of the Lord
units.note: every human summoned, and Israel's horn of salvation

verses.text: kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all rulers of the earth,
young men and maidens, old and young together.
Let them praise the name of the LORD, for His name alone is exalted; His splendor is above the earth and the heavens.
He has raised up a {{qeren|horn}} for His people, the praise of all His saints, of Israel, a people near to Him. {{haleluyah|Hallelujah}}!

haleluyah.gloss: An imperative call, praise the LORD. It opens and closes each of the last five psalms of the Psalter, framing the whole collection's final movement as one continuous shout of praise.

tsava.gloss: Host or army; the ranks of heavenly beings who serve and worship God, summoned here alongside sun, moon, and stars to praise their Maker.

qeren.gloss: Horn, a symbol of strength and victory drawn from an animal's horn. The LORD raises up a horn, a rescuer king, for His people; Zechariah's song applies the same image directly to Jesus, a horn of salvation, Luke 1:69.

basic.context: This psalm summons the entire created order, from angels and stars to sea creatures, weather, mountains, animals, and every class of human being, to praise the LORD. Its reason is twofold, He created and established them by His command, and His name alone is exalted above earth and heaven.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a hymn of universal praise. The command for non-speaking creation to praise God is not literal speech but a poetic summons, calling humanity, the one part of creation with a voice, to give words to what the rest of creation silently displays.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens and closes with haleluyah. Verse 2 summons the tsava, the heavenly hosts, alongside the angels. Verse 14 declares that God has raised up a qeren, a horn, strength and a rescuer, for His people, an image the New Testament applies directly to Jesus.
basic.typology: The horn raised up for God's people, verse 14, anticipates Zechariah's prophecy over his son John the Baptist, that God has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of His servant David, Luke 1:69, a horn fulfilled in Jesus the Messiah.
basic.christ: Colossians 1:16-17 identifies Christ as the one by whom and for whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together, the very truth this psalm celebrates in verses 5-6. He is also the horn of salvation raised up for God's people, Luke 1:69, and the one before whom, with the Lamb, all creation will finally sing, Revelation 5:13.
basic.god: God is Creator and Sustainer of everything that exists, in the heavens and on the earth, established by His command and decree. His name alone is exalted above earth and heaven, and He has drawn Israel near to Himself, raising up strength and salvation for His people.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 1:16-17 identifies Christ as Creator and Sustainer of all things, matching verses 5-6. Luke 1:69 applies this psalm's image of a raised-up horn directly to Jesus. Revelation 5:13 pictures the psalm's summons fulfilled, every creature in heaven and on earth praising God and the Lamb.
basic.oneStory: All creation exists to display God's glory and, through human voices, to praise its Maker. That praise finds its Redeemer and its center in Christ, through whom all things were made, and who is Himself the horn of salvation raised up for God's people, until the day every creature joins the unending song.

context.literary: The third of the Psalter's closing Hallelujah psalms, built as a sandwich, praising angels frame the beginning, verses 1-2, worshiping people frame the conclusion, verses 11-14, and non-speaking creation, stars, weather, mountains, and animals, fills the middle, verses 3-10.
context.historical: Likely used in corporate worship of the Second Temple period, a time with no crisis named, allowing its sweeping, timeless summons to be sung by any generation, and standing against surrounding cultures that worshiped sun, sea, and sky as gods in themselves.
context.canonical: Anticipates Genesis 1's creation by divine command, echoes Job 38-41's roll call of creatures under God's authority, and points toward Revelation 5:13's vision of every creature in heaven and on earth joining the praise of God and the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon the entire created order, heaven and earth alike, to praise the LORD as its Creator and Sustainer, and to remind God's own people, Israel, that their special nearness to Him is itself cause for praise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm poetically describes all creation as praising God through its very existence and order; it prescribes for human worshippers, the one part of creation capable of speech, the ongoing duty and joy of putting that praise into words.

debates.question: Can non-speaking creation, stars, weather, animals, actually be said to praise God, or is this purely poetic language?

views.label: Purely poetic personification, with humans as the only true worshippers
views.case: Stars and storms have no consciousness or will; the summons is a literary device calling attention to creation's order and beauty as evidence for human praise, similar to the heavens declaring God's glory in Psalm 19:1-4, which explicitly notes there is no literal speech.

views.label: A real, though non-verbal, praise inherent in creation's obedient existence and order
views.case: Verses 5-6 ground the summons in creation's obedience to God's command and decree; simply by existing and functioning as designed, creation renders God the praise of faithful obedience, even without words, a form of glory humans then give voice to.

debates.bearing: Both views agree that humans alone can verbally articulate creation's praise; the difference is whether creation's contribution is merely evidence prompting human praise, or itself a real, non-verbal form of glory to God that human words complete.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's frame, haleluyah, calls for praise from beginning to end. Verse 2 names the tsava, heavenly hosts, alongside the angels, part of the summons from the heights. Verse 14 declares that God has raised up a qeren, horn, image of strength and rescue, for His people, later applied directly to Jesus in Luke 1:69.

typology.type: The heavens and all creation created and established by God's command, verses 5-6
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, by whom and for whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together, Colossians 1:16-17.

typology.type: The horn raised up for God's people, verse 14
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, the horn of salvation raised up in the house of David, Luke 1:69.

study.christ: Christ is the one by whom and for whom all creation, summoned to praise in this psalm, was made and is sustained, Colossians 1:16-17. He is the horn of salvation this psalm anticipates, raised up for God's people in the house of David, Luke 1:69, and He is the Lamb before whom, with God on the throne, every creature in heaven and on earth will finally sing, Revelation 5:13, the ultimate fulfillment of this psalm's universal summons.
study.god: God is Creator and Sustainer of the whole cosmos, angels, stars, weather, mountains, animals, and every class of human being, established by His command and an unbreakable decree. His name alone is exalted above earth and heaven, and He has drawn Israel near to Himself, raising up strength and salvation for His people.

crossReferences.note: Identifies Christ as the one by whom and for whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together, matching this psalm's celebration of creation by divine command.
Zechariah's song applies this psalm's image of a raised-up horn directly to Jesus, a horn of salvation in the house of David.
Pictures the fulfillment of this psalm's summons, every creature in heaven and on earth, and in the sea, praising God and the Lamb.

study.oneStory: All creation, seen and unseen, exists to display God's glory and, through human voices, to praise its Maker. That praise finds its center and its Redeemer in Christ, through whom all things were made and in whom all things hold together, and who is Himself the horn of salvation raised up for God's people, until the day every creature joins the unending song before God and the Lamb.

sermon.title: He Commanded and They Were Created
sermon.why: Piper opens a series on redemptive history with Psalm 148:5, He commanded and they were created, unpacking the doctrine of creation and its implications, that God owns, purposes, and sustains all He has made, the very truth this psalm summons all creation to praise Him for.

quiz.q: Who is summoned to praise in verses 1-4?

options.text: The heavens, the highest places, all His angels and heavenly hosts, sun, moon, and shining stars.
options.note: These verses summon the whole heavenly realm to praise before the psalm turns to earth.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the temple.
options.note: The summons here is cosmic, angels and heavenly bodies, not limited to the priesthood.

options.text: Only the kings and rulers of the earth.
options.note: Kings and rulers appear later, in verse 11, not in this opening summons from the heavens.

options.text: The heavens in general terms, without any specific beings named.
options.note: The psalm is quite specific, naming angels, hosts, sun, moon, and stars by name.

quiz.q: Why must creation praise the LORD, according to verses 5-6?

options.text: Because He commanded and they were created, and He established them by a decree that will never pass away.
options.note: Creation's existence and stability are grounded entirely in God's command and decree.

options.text: Because creation evolved into its present form by chance.
options.note: The text explicitly attributes creation to God's command, not chance.

options.text: Because creation praises itself, apart from any Creator.
options.note: The praise is directed to the LORD as the one who commanded creation into being.

options.text: Because creation was made long ago but is no longer sustained by God.
options.note: Verse 6 stresses that God established creation forever and ever, ongoing sustaining, not a one-time act now abandoned.

quiz.q: What does verse 14 say the LORD has done for His people?

options.text: He has raised up a horn for them, the praise of all His saints, of Israel, a people near to Him.
options.note: This closing verse names Israel's special nearness to God and the strength, or rescuer, He has raised up for them.

options.text: He has abandoned them to their enemies.
options.note: The verse describes the opposite, God raising up strength and drawing His people near.

options.text: He has raised up a horn for every nation equally, without distinction.
options.note: The verse specifically names Israel as the people near to Him, not every nation without distinction.

options.text: He has given them wealth but withheld His presence.
options.note: The verse specifically emphasizes nearness to God, not merely material blessing.

quiz.q: How does Luke 1:69 connect to this psalm's image of a horn?

options.text: Zechariah's song applies the same image directly to Jesus, God has raised up a horn of salvation for us.
options.note: Luke 1:69 draws directly on this Old Testament image of a horn as a rescuer king.

options.text: Luke 1:69 rejects the image of a horn entirely.
options.note: Luke 1:69 embraces and fulfills the image rather than rejecting it.

options.text: The horn in Luke 1:69 refers only to a literal musical instrument.
options.note: Both texts use horn as a symbol of strength and rescue, not a literal musical instrument.

options.text: The connection is possible but widely disputed among scholars.
options.note: The verbal and thematic parallel is a well recognized, direct scriptural echo.

quiz.q: What is the range of humanity summoned to praise in verses 11-12?

options.text: Kings and all peoples, princes and rulers, young men and maidens, old and young together.
options.note: The summons spans every social rank and every age, leaving no one excluded.

options.text: Only the wealthy and powerful.
options.note: The text specifically includes young and old, common people alongside rulers.

options.text: Only the priests and Levites.
options.note: The summons is to all humanity, not a specific religious class.

options.text: Kings and rulers only, with the common people left out.
options.note: The text explicitly adds all peoples, young men, maidens, old and young, not rulers alone.

questions: This psalm summons the whole created order, angels, stars, weather, animals, and every kind of person, to praise God. What part of creation, or your own life, tends to go silent instead of praising Him?
Verse 14 says God has raised up a horn, a rescuer, for His people. Where do you need to remember that God has already secured your rescue in Christ?
Kings, rulers, young and old alike are called to praise, verses 11-12. Is there a category of person you unconsciously exclude from worship or from the gospel's reach?
If all creation, seen and unseen, exists to display God's glory, how does that reshape the way you see an ordinary part of your day, weather, work, or a walk outside?
This psalm's praise anticipates Revelation 5:13's vision of every creature praising God and the Lamb. How does that future hope change how you worship today?

translationNotes.note: The highest places, or the heights, renders a Hebrew phrase for the upper heavens, associated with God's own presence and the angels.
Waters above the skies reflects the ancient Hebrew picture of the created order in Genesis 1:6-8, waters separated by an expanse, here summoned along with everything else to praise their Maker.
Horn renders qeren, an image of strength drawn from an animal's horn; it becomes a title for the promised rescuer king, as in Luke 1:69.

Footnote markers [a] and [b] stripped. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's message 'He Commanded and They Were Created' at desiringgod.org, scripture Psalm 148:5.
